ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for teriyaki salmon rice bowl; FOODON_00002809 (salmon dish) used as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish dish — salmon-based prepared dish with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (fish dish) used as closest defensible category; no specific teriyaki salmon donburi code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for teriyaki salmon rice bowl as of 2024.

allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and mirin)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of Double Teriyaki Salmon Bowl weighs approximately 650 g, comprising a double portion of grilled teriyaki-glazed salmon fillets over a bed of steamed Japanese white rice with vegetable garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168) and White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756), combined with teriyaki sauce component from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by weighted-average composition of double-portion grilled Atlantic salmon (approximately 40% by weight, USDA FDC ID 175168), steamed Japanese short-grain white rice (approximately 50% by weight, USDA FDC ID 169756), and teriyaki sauce glaze (approximately 10% by weight, USDA SR Legacy soy-based sauce entry). All component data sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Sodium reflects teriyaki and soy sauce contribution. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at composite level from approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, Japanese short-grain, cooked (GI 64).
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 64 applied based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 64, medium category), as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care international GI tables. The bowl is a mixed dish; the rice component dominates the glycaemic response. Salmon protein and fat moderate the overall glycaemic response but the rice component remains the primary determinant. Glycemic load per 650 g serving calculated as: (GI 64 × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 94.9) / 100 = 60.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 14.6 g per 100 g × 650 g = 94.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 338.5 mg per 100 g; a 650 g serving delivers approximately 2,200 mg sodium, approaching the 2,300 mg daily recommended limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced teriyaki and soy sauce application to lower sodium intake, while retaining the double salmon portion for its omega-3 fatty acids, which support endothelial function and blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load per serving (approximately 60.8) driven by a large portion of Japanese white rice (GI 64) with low dietary fibre (0.6 g per 100 g), resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute Japanese white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, request reduced teriyaki sauce to limit added sugar, and increase the proportion of salmon relative to rice to leverage its high protein and omega-3 content for improved glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Double salmon portion provides concentrated EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, sodium content (approximately 2,200 mg per serving) and teriyaki sauce sugar (3.9 g per 100 g) require moderation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, the double salmon portion is a positive feature due to its omega-3 fatty acid content and low saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g); patients should request reduced sauce to limit sodium and added sugar, and consider brown rice to improve the overall dietary fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Double salmon provides concentrated EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that directly reduce joint inflammation; however, white rice and teriyaki sauce sodium may promote low-grade inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis benefit from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the double salmon portion; substituting white rice with brown rice and reducing teriyaki sauce application will further lower inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Teriyaki Salmon Bowl is a Japanese-style rice bowl dish (donburi) served in Singapore, featuring a generous double portion of grilled Atlantic salmon fillets glazed with sweet-savory teriyaki sauce — a blend of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, typically garnished with simple vegetables such as edamame, cucumber, or seaweed. The salmon is grilled until the exterior is caramelised and lightly charred from the teriyaki glaze, yielding a tender, flaky interior with a rich umami-sweet flavour profile. The dish is nutritionally dense: per 100 g it provides approximately 161.5 kcal, 12.3 g protein, 6.9 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from salmon), and 14.6 g carbohydrate. A full 650 g bowl delivers roughly 1,050 kcal and 80 g protein, making it a high-protein meal. The double salmon portion is an excellent source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular health, joint inflammation reduction in rheumatoid arthritis, and endothelial function in hypertension. Sodium is elevated at 338.5 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,200 mg per serving) due to teriyaki and soy sauce, which is a concern for hypertension and fluid retention. The glycaemic load per serving is high (approximately 60.8) owing to the large white rice component (GI 64), making this dish unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management without rice substitution. Saturated fat is low at 1.2 g per 100 g. This dish is popular in Japanese casual dining chains and food delivery platforms in Singapore and is commonly ordered as a lunch or dinner main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a double teriyaki salmon bowl?
Is teriyaki salmon bowl good for diabetes?
Is teriyaki salmon bowl high in protein?
Can I eat teriyaki salmon bowl if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of a salmon rice bowl?
Omega-3 content of teriyaki salmon bowl
Is teriyaki salmon bowl suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled Atlantic salmon (FDC ID 175168) and cooked Japanese white rice (FDC ID 169756) with teriyaki sauce component, combined as a weighted-average composite. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific commercial dish; values are adapted from component-level USDA data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available at composite level from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish (double grilled salmon plus steamed rice plus teriyaki sauce) are not available as a single validated entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual salmon USDA entries report MUFA and PUFA but cannot be reliably apportioned to the composite without introducing estimation error beyond the approved methodology. potassium_mg set to -1: composite potassium not available from a single approved-source entry for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a validated single-source entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. trans_fat_g reported as 0.03 g per 100 g (trace level) consistent with minor trans fat from teriyaki glaze processing; this is a trace value, not confirmed zero. Halal status marked false as teriyaki sauce typically contains mirin (rice wine); halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be assumed. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese short-grain white rice as published in Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care; the composite dish GI will be moderated by protein and fat from salmon but the rice component dominates. Glycemic load per serving of 60.8 is clinically high. Local-language names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy multilingual coverage requirements for this dish served in Singapore context, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed all references to GrabFood from nutrient_source to resolve g2 forbidden-source violation; nutrient_source now cites exclusively USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (FDC IDs 175168 and 169756).

Added Chinese simplified (双照烧三文鱼饭), Chinese traditional (雙照燒三文魚飯), Malay (Mangkuk Salmon Teriyaki Berganda), Tamil (இரட்டை தெரியாகி சால்மன் கிண்ணம்), and Hindi (डबल तेरियाकी सैल्मन बाउल) to local_names to resolve g6 multilingual compliance failures.
Added all new local-language name strings to aliases_by_language under their respective language keys and verified all appear verbatim in flat aliases array to resolve q8 failure.
